fact: January 6 is exactly 11 days before the halfway point of winter in the Northern Hemisphere - making it the perfect time for winter animals to be at their fluffiest!
In Antarctica, January 6 falls during the peak of summer, when the sun never sets and penguins are busy raising their chicks in 24-hour daylight.
The sunrise on January 6 happens about 30 seconds later each day than it did in early December, but sunset keeps getting later much faster - so days are actually getting longer!
January 6 is the 6th day of the year, which means there are exactly 359 days left (or 360 in leap years) - that's almost a full circle of days!
In northern regions, tree sap is typically frozen solid by January 6, making trees like frozen popsicles during the coldest part of winter!
Many hibernating animals like bears and groundhogs are usually in their deepest sleep phase around January 6, with their heart rates slowed to just 8-10 beats per minute.
When there's a full moon on January 6, it often appears extra bright because winter air is clearer and drier than summer air.
In the Southern Hemisphere, January 6 is typically prime time for cicadas to emerge and make their loudest buzzing sounds - some can be heard from over a mile away!
Snow that falls on January 6 in cold climates often makes a different crunching sound than December snow because the colder air temperature makes the crystals form differently.
January 6 falls during the time when Earth is actually closest to the Sun in its orbit - only about 91.4 million miles away instead of the usual 93 million!
In tropical regions, January 6 typically marks the time when many fruit bats are most active, flying up to 30 miles per night to find the ripest mangoes and figs.
